    23â†’
    24â†’func prepareFinalInputVariableValue(addr addrs.AbsInputVariableInstance, raw *InputValue, cfg *configs.Variable) (cty.Value, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
    25â†’	var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics
    26â†’
    27â†’	convertTy := cfg.ConstraintType
    28â†’	log.Printf("[TRACE] prepareFinalInputVariableValue: preparing %s", addr)
    29â†’
    30â†’	var defaultVal cty.Value
    31â†’	if cfg.Default != cty.NilVal {
    32â†’		log.Printf("[TRACE] prepareFinalInputVariableValue: %s has a default value", addr)
    33â†’		var err error
    34â†’		defaultVal, err = convert.Convert(cfg.Default, convertTy)
    35â†’		if err != nil {
    36â†’			// Validation of the declaration should typically catch this,
    37â†’			// but we'll check it here too to be robust.
    38â†’			diags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{
    39â†’				Severity: hcl.DiagError,
    40â†’				Summary:  "Invalid default value for module argument",
    41â†’				Detail: fmt.Sprintf(
    42â†’					"The default value for variable %q is incompatible with its type constraint: %s.",
    43â†’					cfg.Name, err,
    44â†’				),
    45â†’				Subject: &cfg.DeclRange,
    46â†’			})
    47â†’			// We'll return a placeholder unknown value to avoid producing
    48â†’			// redundant downstream errors.
    49â†’			return cty.UnknownVal(cfg.Type), diags
    50â†’		}
    51â†’	}
    52â†’
    53â†’	var sourceRange tfdiags.SourceRange
    54â†’	var nonFileSource string
    55â†’	if raw.HasSourceRange() {
    56â†’		sourceRange = raw.SourceRange
    57â†’	} else {
    58â†’		// If the value came from a place that isn't a file and thus doesn't
    59â†’		// have its own source range, we'll use the declaration range as
    60â†’		// our source range and generate some slightly different error
    61â†’		// messages.
    62â†’		sourceRange = tfdiags.SourceRangeFromHCL(cfg.DeclRange)
    63â†’		switch raw.SourceType {
    64â†’		case ValueFromCLIArg:
    65â†’			nonFileSource = fmt.Sprintf("set using -var=\"%s=...\"", addr.Variable.Name)
    66â†’		case ValueFromEnvVar:
    67â†’			nonFileSource = fmt.Sprintf("set using the TF_VAR_%s environment variable", addr.Variable.Name)
    68â†’		case ValueFromInput:
    69â†’			nonFileSource = "set using an interactive prompt"
    70â†’		default:
    71â†’			nonFileSource = "set from outside of the configuration"
    72â†’		}
    73â†’	}
    74â†’
    75â†’	given := raw.Value
    76â†’	if given == cty.NilVal { // The variable wasn't set at all (even to null)
    77â†’		log.Printf("[TRACE] prepareFinalInputVariableValue: %s has no defined value", addr)
    78â†’		if cfg.Required() {
    79â†’			// NOTE: The CLI layer typically checks for itself whether all of
    80â†’			// the required _root_ module variables are set, which would
    81â†’			// mask this error with a more specific one that refers to the
    82â†’			// CLI features for setting such variables. We can get here for
    83â†’			// child module variables, though.
    84â†’			log.Printf("[ERROR] prepareFinalInputVariableValue: %s is required but is not set", addr)
    85â†’			diags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{
    86â†’				Severity: hcl.DiagError,
    87â†’				Summary:  `Required variable not set`,
    88â†’				Detail:   fmt.Sprintf(`The variable %q is required, but is not set.`, addr.Variable.Name),
    89â†’				Subject:  cfg.DeclRange.Ptr(),
    90â†’			})
    91â†’			// We'll return a placeholder unknown value to avoid producing
    92â†’			// redundant downstream errors.
    93â†’			return cty.UnknownVal(cfg.Type), diags
    94â†’		}
    95â†’
    96â†’		given = defaultVal // must be set, because we checked above that the variable isn't required
    97â†’	}
    98â†’
    99â†’	// Apply defaults from the variable's type constraint to the converted value,
   100â†’	// unless the converted value is null. We do not apply defaults to top-level
   101â†’	// null values, as doing so could prevent assigning null to a nullable
   102â†’	// variable.
   103â†’	if cfg.TypeDefaults != nil && !given.IsNull() {
   104â†’		given = cfg.TypeDefaults.Apply(given)
   105â†’	}
   106â†’
   107â†’	val, err := convert.Convert(given, convertTy)
   108â†’	if err != nil {
   109â†’		log.Printf("[ERROR] prepareFinalInputVariableValue: %s has unsuitable type\n  got:  %s\n  want: %s", addr, given.Type(), convertTy)
   110â†’		var detail string
   111â†’		var subject *hcl.Range
   112â†’		if nonFileSource != "" {
   113â†’			detail = fmt.Sprintf(
   114â†’				"Unsuitable value for %s %s: %s.",
   115â†’				addr, nonFileSource, err,
   116â†’			)
   117â†’			subject = cfg.DeclRange.Ptr()
   118â†’		} else {
   119â†’			detail = fmt.Sprintf(
   120â†’				"The given value is not suitable for %s declared at %s: %s.",
   121â†’				addr, cfg.DeclRange.String(), err,
   122â†’			)
   123â†’			subject = sourceRange.ToHCL().Ptr()
   124â†’
   125â†’			// In some workflows, the operator running terraform does not have access to the variables
   126â†’			// themselves. They are for example stored in encrypted files that will be used by the CI toolset
   127â†’			// and not by the operator directly. In such a case, the failing secret value should not be
   128â†’			// displayed to the operator
   129â†’			if cfg.Sensitive {
   130â†’				detail = fmt.Sprintf(
   131â†’					"The given value is not suitable for %s, which is sensitive: %s. Invalid value defined at %s.",
   132â†’					addr, err, sourceRange.ToHCL(),
   133â†’				)
   134â†’				subject = cfg.DeclRange.Ptr()
   135â†’			}
   136â†’		}
   137â†’
   138â†’		diags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{
   139â†’			Severity: hcl.DiagError,
   140â†’			Summary:  "Invalid value for input variable",
   141â†’			Detail:   detail,
   142â†’			Subject:  subject,
   143â†’		})
   144â†’		// We'll return a placeholder unknown value to avoid producing
   145â†’		// redundant downstream errors.
   146â†’		return cty.UnknownVal(cfg.Type), diags
   147â†’	}
   148â†’
   149â†’	// By the time we get here, we know:
   150â†’	// - val matches the variable's type constraint
   151â†’	// - val is definitely not cty.NilVal, but might be a null value if the given was already null.
   152â†’	//
   153â†’	// That means we just need to handle the case where the value is null,
   154â†’	// which might mean we need to use the default value, or produce an error.
   155â†’	//
   156â†’	// For historical reasons we do this only for a "non-nullable" variable.
   157â†’	// Nullable variables just appear as null if they were set to null,
   158â†’	// regardless of any default value.
   159â†’	if val.IsNull() && !cfg.Nullable {
   160â†’		log.Printf("[TRACE] prepareFinalInputVariableValue: %s is defined as null", addr)
   161â†’		if defaultVal != cty.NilVal {
   162â†’			val = defaultVal
   163â†’		} else {
   164â†’			log.Printf("[ERROR] prepareFinalInputVariableValue: %s is non-nullable but set to null, and is required", addr)
   165â†’			if nonFileSource != "" {
   166â†’				diags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{
   167â†’					Severity: hcl.DiagError,
   168â†’					Summary:  `Required variable not set`,
   169â†’					Detail: fmt.Sprintf(
   170â†’						"Unsuitable value for %s %s: required variable may not be set to null.",
   171â†’						addr, nonFileSource,
   172â†’					),
   173â†’					Subject: cfg.DeclRange.Ptr(),
   174â†’				})
   175â†’			} else {
   176â†’				diags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{
   177â†’					Severity: hcl.DiagError,
   178â†’					Summary:  `Required variable not set`,
   179â†’					Detail: fmt.Sprintf(
   180â†’						"The given value is not suitable for %s defined at %s: required variable may not be set to null.",
   181â†’						addr, cfg.DeclRange.String(),
   182â†’					),
   183â†’					Subject: sourceRange.ToHCL().Ptr(),
   184â†’				})
   185â†’			}
   186â†’			// Stub out our return value so that the semantic checker doesn't
   187â†’			// produce redundant downstream errors.
   188â†’			val = cty.UnknownVal(cfg.Type)
   189â†’		}
   190â†’	}
   191â†’
   192â†’	if !cfg.Ephemeral {
   193â†’		// An ephemeral input variable always has an ephemeral value inside the
   194â†’		// module, even if the value assigned to it from outside is not. This
   195â†’		// is a useful simplification so that module authors can be explicit
   196â†’		// about what guarantees they are intending to make (regardless of
   197â†’		// current implementation details). Changing the ephemerality of an
   198â†’		// input variable is a breaking change to a module's API.
   199â†’		val = val.Mark(marks.Ephemeral)
   200â†’	} else {
   201â†’		if marks.Contains(val, marks.Ephemeral) {
   202â†’			var subject hcl.Range
   203â†’			if raw.HasSourceRange() {
   204â†’				subject = raw.SourceRange.ToHCL()
   205â†’			} else {
   206â†’				// We shouldn't typically get here for ephemeral values, because
   207â†’				// all of the source types that can represent expressions that
   208â†’				// could potentially produce ephemeral values are those which
   209â†’				// have source locations. This is just here for robustness.
   210â†’				subject = cfg.DeclRange
   211â†’			}
   212â†’
   213â†’			diags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{
   214â†’				Severity: hcl.DiagError,
   215â†’				Summary:  "Ephemeral value not allowed",
   216â†’				Detail:   "This input variable is not declared as accepting a ephemeral values, so it cannot be set to a result derived from an ephemeral value.",
   217â†’				Subject:  subject.Ptr(),
   218â†’			})
   219â†’		}
   220â†’	}
   221â†’
   222â†’	return val, diags
   223â†’}
   224â†’
   225â†’// evalVariableValidations ensures that all of the configured custom validations
   226â†’// for a variable are passing.
   227â†’//
   228â†’// This must be used only after any side-effects that make the value of the
   229â†’// variable available for use in expression evaluation, such as
   230â†’// EvalModuleCallArgument for variables in descendant modules.
   231â†’func evalVariableValidations(addr addrs.AbsInputVariableInstance, ctx EvalContext, rules []*configs.CheckRule, valueRng hcl.Range, validateWalk bool) (diags tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
   232â†’	if len(rules) == 0 {
   233â†’		log.Printf("[TRACE] evalVariableValidations: no validation rules declared for %s, so skipping", addr)
   234â†’		return nil
   235â†’	}
   236â†’	log.Printf("[TRACE] evalVariableValidations: validating %s", addr)
   237â†’
   238â†’	checkState := ctx.Checks()
   239â†’	if !checkState.ConfigHasChecks(addr.ConfigCheckable()) {
   240â†’		// We have nothing to do if this object doesn't have any checks,
   241â†’		// but the "rules" slice should agree that we don't.
   242â†’		if ct := len(rules); ct != 0 {
   243â†’			panic(fmt.Sprintf("check state says that %s should have no rules, but it has %d", addr, ct))
   244â†’		}
   245â†’		return diags
   246â†’	}
   247â†’
   248â†’	// We'll build just one evaluation context covering the data needed by
   249â†’	// all of the rules together, since that'll minimize lock contention
   250â†’	// on the state, plan, etc.
   251â†’	scope := ctx.EvaluationScope(nil, nil, EvalDataForNoInstanceKey)
   252â†’	var refs []*addrs.Reference
   253â†’	for _, rule := range rules {
   254â†’		condRefs, moreDiags := langrefs.ReferencesInExpr(addrs.ParseRef, rule.Condition)
   255â†’		diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
   256â†’		msgRefs, moreDiags := langrefs.ReferencesInExpr(addrs.ParseRef, rule.ErrorMessage)
   257â†’		diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
   258â†’		refs = append(refs, condRefs...)
   259â†’		refs = append(refs, msgRefs...)
   260â†’	}
   261â†’	if diags.HasErrors() {
   262â†’		// If any of the references were invalid then evaluating the expressions
   263â†’		// will duplicate those errors, so we'll bail out early.
   264â†’		return diags
   265â†’	}
   266â†’	hclCtx, moreDiags := scope.EvalContext(refs)
   267â†’	diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
   268â†’	if moreDiags.HasErrors() {
   269â†’		return diags
   270â†’	}
   271â†’
   272â†’	// HACK: Historically we manually built a very constrained hcl.EvalContext
   273â†’	// here, which only included the value of the one specific input variable
   274â†’	// we're validating, since we didn't yet support referring to anything
   275â†’	// else. That accidentally bypassed our rule that input variables are
   276â†’	// always unknown during the validate walk, and thus accidentally created
   277â†’	// a useful behavior of actually checking constant-only values against
   278â†’	// their validation rules just during "terraform validate", rather than
   279â†’	// having to run "terraform plan".
   280â†’	//
   281â†’	// Although that behavior was accidental, it makes simple validation rules
   282â†’	// more useful and is protected by compatibility promises, and so we'll
   283â†’	// fake it here by overwriting the unknown value that scope.EvalContext
   284â†’	// will have inserted with a possibly-more-known value using the same
   285â†’	// strategy our special code used to use.
   286â†’	ourVal := ctx.NamedValues().GetInputVariableValue(addr)
   287â†’	if ourVal != cty.NilVal {
   288â†’		// (it would be weird for ourVal to be nil here, but we'll tolerate it
   289â†’		// because it was scope.EvalContext's responsibility to check for the
   290â†’		// absent final value, and even if it didn't we'll just get an
   291â†’		// evaluation error when evaluating the expressions below anyway.)
   292â†’
   293â†’		// Our goal here is to make sure that a reference to the variable
   294â†’		// we're checking will evaluate to ourVal, regardless of what else
   295â†’		// scope.EvalContext might have put in the variables table.
   296â†’		if hclCtx.Variables == nil {
   297â†’			hclCtx.Variables = make(map[string]cty.Value)
   298â†’		}
   299â†’		if varsVal, ok := hclCtx.Variables["var"]; ok {
   300â†’			// Unfortunately we need to unpack and repack the object here,
   301â†’			// because cty values are immutable.
   302â†’			attrs := varsVal.AsValueMap()
   303â†’			attrs[addr.Variable.Name] = ourVal
   304â†’			hclCtx.Variables["var"] = cty.ObjectVal(attrs)
   305â†’		} else {
   306â†’			hclCtx.Variables["var"] = cty.ObjectVal(map[string]cty.Value{
   307â†’				addr.Variable.Name: ourVal,
   308â†’			})
   309â†’		}
   310â†’	}
   311â†’
   312â†’	for ix, validation := range rules {
   313â†’		result, ruleDiags := evalVariableValidation(validation, hclCtx, valueRng, addr, ix, validateWalk)
   314â†’		diags = diags.Append(ruleDiags)
   315â†’
   316â†’		log.Printf("[TRACE] evalVariableValidations: %s status is now %s", addr, result.Status)
   317â†’		if result.Status == checks.StatusFail {
   318â†’			checkState.ReportCheckFailure(addr, addrs.InputValidation, ix, result.FailureMessage)
   319â†’		} else {
   320â†’			checkState.ReportCheckResult(addr, addrs.InputValidation, ix, result.Status)
   321â†’		}
   322â†’	}
   323â†’
   324â†’	return diags
   325â†’}
   326â†’
   327â†’func evalVariableValidation(validation *configs.CheckRule, hclCtx *hcl.EvalContext, valueRng hcl.Range, addr addrs.AbsInputVariableInstance, ix int, validateWalk bool) (checkResult, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
   328â†’	const errInvalidCondition = "Invalid variable validation result"
   329â†’	const errInvalidValue = "Invalid value for variable"
   330â†’	var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics
   331â†’
   332â†’	result, moreDiags := validation.Condition.Value(hclCtx)
   333â†’	diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
   334â†’	errorValue, errorDiags := validation.ErrorMessage.Value(hclCtx)
   335â†’
   336â†’	// The following error handling is a workaround to preserve backwards
   337â†’	// compatibility. Due to an implementation quirk, all prior versions of
   338â†’	// Terraform would treat error messages specified using JSON
   339â†’	// configuration syntax (.tf.json) as string literals, even if they
   340â†’	// contained the "${" template expression operator. This behaviour did
   341â†’	// not match that of HCL configuration syntax, where a template
   342â†’	// expression would result in a validation error.
   343â†’	//
   344â†’	// As a result, users writing or generating JSON configuration syntax
   345â†’	// may have specified error messages which are invalid template
   346â†’	// expressions. As we add support for error message expressions, we are
   347â†’	// unable to perfectly distinguish between these two cases.
   348â†’	//
   349â†’	// To ensure that we don't break backwards compatibility, we have the
   350â†’	// below fallback logic if the error message fails to evaluate. This
   351â†’	// should only have any effect for JSON configurations. The gohcl
   352â†’	// DecodeExpression function behaves differently when the source of the
   353â†’	// expression is a JSON configuration file and a nil context is passed.
   354â†’	if errorDiags.HasErrors() {
   355â†’		// Attempt to decode the expression as a string literal. Passing
   356â†’		// nil as the context forces a JSON syntax string value to be
   357â†’		// interpreted as a string literal.
   358â†’		var errorString string
   359â†’		moreErrorDiags := gohcl.DecodeExpression(validation.ErrorMessage, nil, &errorString)
   360â†’		if !moreErrorDiags.HasErrors() {
   361â†’			// Decoding succeeded, meaning that this is a JSON syntax
   362â†’			// string value. We rewrap that as a cty value to allow later
   363â†’			// decoding to succeed.
   364â†’			errorValue = cty.StringVal(errorString)
   365â†’
   366â†’			// This warning diagnostic explains this odd behaviour, while
   367â†’			// giving us an escape hatch to change this to a hard failure
   368â†’			// in some future Terraform 1.x version.
   369â†’			errorDiags = hcl.Diagnostics{
   370â†’				&hcl.Diagnostic{
   371â†’					Severity:    hcl.DiagWarning,
   372â†’					Summary:     "Validation error message expression is invalid",
   373â†’					Detail:      fmt.Sprintf("The error message provided could not be evaluated as an expression, so Terraform is interpreting it as a string literal.\n\nIn future versions of Terraform, this will be considered an error. Please file a GitHub issue if this would break your workflow.\n\n%s", errorDiags.Error()),
   374â†’					Subject:     validation.ErrorMessage.Range().Ptr(),
   375â†’					Context:     validation.DeclRange.Ptr(),
   376â†’					Expression:  validation.ErrorMessage,
   377â†’					EvalContext: hclCtx,
   378â†’				},
   379â†’			}
   380â†’		}
   381â†’
   382â†’		// We want to either report the original diagnostics if the
   383â†’		// fallback failed, or the warning generated above if it succeeded.
   384â†’		diags = diags.Append(errorDiags)
   385â†’	}
   386â†’
   387â†’	if diags.HasErrors() {
   388â†’		log.Printf("[TRACE] evalVariableValidations: %s rule %s check rule evaluation failed: %s", addr, validation.DeclRange, diags.Err().Error())
   389â†’	}
   390â†’	if !result.IsKnown() {
   391â†’		log.Printf("[TRACE] evalVariableValidations: %s rule %s condition value is unknown, so skipping validation for now", addr, validation.DeclRange)
   392â†’
   393â†’		return checkResult{Status: checks.StatusUnknown}, diags // We'll wait until we've learned more, then.
   394â†’	}
   395â†’	if result.IsNull() {
   396â†’		diags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{
   397â†’			Severity:    hcl.DiagError,
   398â†’			Summary:     errInvalidCondition,
   399â†’			Detail:      "Validation condition expression must return either true or false, not null.",
   400â†’			Subject:     validation.Condition.Range().Ptr(),
   401â†’			Expression:  validation.Condition,
   402â†’			EvalContext: hclCtx,
   403â†’		})
   404â†’		return checkResult{Status: checks.StatusError}, diags
   405â†’	}
   406â†’	var err error
   407â†’	result, err = convert.Convert(result, cty.Bool)
   408â†’	if err != nil {
   409â†’		diags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{
   410â†’			Severity:    hcl.DiagError,
   411â†’			Summary:     errInvalidCondition,
   412â†’			Detail:      fmt.Sprintf("Invalid validation condition result value: %s.", tfdiags.FormatError(err)),
   413â†’			Subject:     validation.Condition.Range().Ptr(),
   414â†’			Expression:  validation.Condition,
   415â†’			EvalContext: hclCtx,
   416â†’		})
   417â†’		return checkResult{Status: checks.StatusError}, diags
   418â†’	}
   419â†’
   420â†’	// Validation condition may be marked if the input variable is bound to
   421â†’	// a sensitive value. This is irrelevant to the validation process, so
   422â†’	// we discard the marks now.
   423â†’	result, _ = result.Unmark()
   424â†’	status := checks.StatusForCtyValue(result)
   425â†’
   426â†’	if status != checks.StatusFail {
   427â†’		return checkResult{Status: status}, diags
   428â†’	}
   429â†’
   430â†’	if !errorValue.IsKnown() {
   431â†’		if validateWalk {
   432â†’			log.Printf("[DEBUG] evalVariableValidations: %s rule %s error_message value is unknown, so skipping validation for now", addr, validation.DeclRange)
   433â†’			return checkResult{Status: checks.StatusUnknown}, diags
   434â†’		}
   435â†’
   436â†’		diags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{
   437â†’			Severity:    hcl.DiagError,
   438â†’			Summary:     "Invalid error message",
   439â†’			Detail:      "Unsuitable value for error message: expression refers to values that won't be known until the apply phase.",
   440â†’			Subject:     validation.ErrorMessage.Range().Ptr(),
   441â†’			Expression:  validation.ErrorMessage,
   442â†’			EvalContext: hclCtx,
   443â†’			Extra:       diagnosticCausedByUnknown(true),
   444â†’		})
   445â†’		return checkResult{
   446â†’			Status: checks.StatusError,
   447â†’		}, diags
   448â†’	}
   449â†’
   450â†’	var errorMessage string
   451â†’	if !errorDiags.HasErrors() && !errorValue.IsNull() {
   452â†’		var err error
   453â†’		errorValue, err = convert.Convert(errorValue, cty.String)
   454â†’		if err != nil {
   455â†’			diags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{
   456â†’				Severity:    hcl.DiagError,
   457â†’				Summary:     "Invalid error message",
   458â†’				Detail:      fmt.Sprintf("Unsuitable value for error message: %s.", tfdiags.FormatError(err)),
   459â†’				Subject:     validation.ErrorMessage.Range().Ptr(),
   460â†’				Expression:  validation.ErrorMessage,
   461â†’				EvalContext: hclCtx,
   462â†’			})
   463â†’		} else {
   464â†’			if marks.Has(errorValue, marks.Sensitive) {
   465â†’				diags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{
   466â†’					Severity: hcl.DiagError,
   467â†’
   468â†’					Summary: "Error message refers to sensitive values",
   469â†’					Detail: `The error expression used to explain this condition refers to sensitive values. Terraform will not display the resulting message.
   470â†’
   471â†’You can correct this by removing references to sensitive values, or by carefully using the nonsensitive() function if the expression will not reveal the sensitive data.`,
   472â†’
   473â†’					Subject:     validation.ErrorMessage.Range().Ptr(),
   474â†’					Expression:  validation.ErrorMessage,
   475â†’					EvalContext: hclCtx,
   476â†’				})
   477â†’				errorMessage = "The error message included a sensitive value, so it will not be displayed."
   478â†’			} else if marks.Has(errorValue, marks.Ephemeral) {
   479â†’				diags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{
   480â†’					Severity: hcl.DiagError,
   481â†’
   482â†’					Summary: "Error message refers to ephemeral values",
   483â†’					Detail: `The error expression used to explain this condition refers to ephemeral values. Terraform will not display the resulting message.
   484â†’
   485â†’You can correct this by removing references to ephemeral values, or by carefully using the ephemeralasnull() function if the expression will not reveal the ephemeral data.`,
   486â†’
   487â†’					Subject:     validation.ErrorMessage.Range().Ptr(),
   488â†’					Expression:  validation.ErrorMessage,
   489â†’					EvalContext: hclCtx,
   490â†’				})
   491â†’				errorMessage = "The error message included a sensitive value, so it will not be displayed."
   492â†’			} else {
   493â†’				errorMessage = strings.TrimSpace(errorValue.AsString())
   494â†’			}
   495â†’		}
   496â†’	}
   497â†’	if errorMessage == "" {
   498â†’		errorMessage = "Failed to evaluate condition error message."
   499â†’	}
   500â†’
   501â†’	diags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{
   502â†’		Severity:    hcl.DiagError,
   503â†’		Summary:     errInvalidValue,
   504â†’		Detail:      fmt.Sprintf("%s\n\nThis was checked by the validation rule at %s.", errorMessage, validation.DeclRange.String()),
   505â†’		Subject:     valueRng.Ptr(),
   506â†’		Expression:  validation.Condition,
   507â†’		EvalContext: hclCtx,
   508â†’		Extra: &addrs.CheckRuleDiagnosticExtra{
   509â†’			CheckRule: addr.CheckRule(addrs.InputValidation, ix),
   510â†’		},
   511â†’	})
   512â†’
   513â†’	return checkResult{
   514â†’		Status:         status,
   515â†’		FailureMessage: errorMessage,
   516â†’	}, diags
   517â†’}
   518â†’

1â†’// Copyright (c) HashiCorp, Inc.

     3â†’
     4â†’package terraform
     5â†’
     6â†’import (
     7â†’	"fmt"
     8â†’	"log"
     9â†’
    10â†’	"github.com/zclconf/go-cty/cty"
    11â†’
    12â†’	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/addrs"
    13â†’	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/collections"
    14â†’	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/configs"
    15â†’	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/lang"
    16â†’	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/plans"
    17â†’	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/providers"
    18â†’	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/states"
    19â†’	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/tfdiags"
    20â†’)
    21â†’
    22â†’// ApplyOpts are options that affect the details of how Terraform will apply a
    23â†’// previously-generated plan.
    24â†’type ApplyOpts struct {
    25â†’	// ExternalProviders is a set of pre-configured provider instances with
    26â†’	// the same purpose as [PlanOpts.ExternalProviders].
    27â†’	//
    28â†’	// Callers must pass providers that are configured in a similar way as
    29â†’	// the providers that were passed when creating the plan that's being
    30â†’	// applied, or the results will be erratic.
    31â†’	ExternalProviders map[addrs.RootProviderConfig]providers.Interface
    32â†’
    33â†’	// SetVariables are the raw values for root module variables as provided
    34â†’	// by the user who is requesting the run, prior to any normalization or
    35â†’	// substitution of defaults. See the documentation for the InputValue
    36â†’	// type for more information on how to correctly populate this.
    37â†’	//
    38â†’	// During the apply phase it's only valid to specify values for input
    39â†’	// values that were declared as ephemeral, because all other input
    40â†’	// values must retain the values that were specified during planning.
    41â†’	SetVariables InputValues
    42â†’}
    43â†’
    44â†’// ApplyOpts creates an [ApplyOpts] with copies of all of the elements that
    45â†’// are expected to propagate from plan to apply when planning and applying
    46â†’// in the same process.
    47â†’//
    48â†’// In practice planning and applying are often separated into two different
    49â†’// executions, in which case callers must retain enough information between
    50â†’// plan and apply to construct an equivalent [ApplyOpts] themselves without
    51â†’// using this function. This is here mainly for convenient internal use such
    52â†’// as in test cases.
    53â†’func (po *PlanOpts) ApplyOpts() *ApplyOpts {
    54â†’	return &ApplyOpts{
    55â†’		ExternalProviders: po.ExternalProviders,
    56â†’	}
    57â†’}
    58â†’
    59â†’// Apply performs the actions described by the given Plan object and returns
    60â†’// the resulting updated state.
    61â†’//
    62â†’// The given configuration *must* be the same configuration that was passed
    63â†’// earlier to Context.Plan in order to create this plan.
    64â†’//
    65â†’// Even if the returned diagnostics contains errors, Apply always returns the
    66â†’// resulting state which is likely to have been partially-updated.
    67â†’//
    68â†’// The [opts] argument may be nil to indicate that no special options are
    69â†’// required. In that case, Apply will use a default set of options. Some
    70â†’// options in [PlanOpts] when creating a plan must be echoed with equivalent
    71â†’// settings during apply, so leaving opts as nil might not be valid for
    72â†’// certain combinations of plan-time options.
    73â†’func (c *Context) Apply(plan *plans.Plan, config *configs.Config, opts *ApplyOpts) (*states.State, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
    74â†’	state, _, diags := c.ApplyAndEval(plan, config, opts)
    75â†’	return state, diags
    76â†’}
    77â†’
    78â†’// ApplyAndEval is like [Context.Apply] except that it additionally makes a
    79â†’// best effort to return a [lang.Scope] which can evaluate expressions in the
    80â†’// root module based on the content of the new state.
    81â†’//
    82â†’// The scope will be nil if the apply process doesn't complete successfully
    83â†’// enough to produce a valid evaluation scope. If the returned state is nil
    84â†’// then the scope will always be nil, but it's also possible for the scope
    85â†’// to be nil even when the state isn't, if the apply didn't complete enough for
    86â†’// the evaluation scope to produce consistent results.
    87â†’func (c *Context) ApplyAndEval(plan *plans.Plan, config *configs.Config, opts *ApplyOpts) (*states.State, *lang.Scope, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
    88â†’	defer c.acquireRun("apply")()
    89â†’	var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics
    90â†’
    91â†’	if plan == nil {
    92â†’		panic("cannot apply nil plan")
    93â†’	}
    94â†’	log.Printf("[DEBUG] Building and walking apply graph for %s plan", plan.UIMode)
    95â†’
    96â†’	if opts == nil {
    97â†’		opts = &ApplyOpts{}
    98â†’	}
    99â†’
   100â†’	if plan.Errored {
   101â†’		diags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(
   102â†’			tfdiags.Error,
   103â†’			"Cannot apply failed plan",
   104â†’			`The given plan is incomplete due to errors during planning, and so it cannot be applied.`,
   105â†’		))
   106â†’		return nil, nil, diags
   107â†’	}
   108â†’	if !plan.Applyable {
   109â†’		if plan.Changes.Empty() {
   110â†’			// If a plan is not applyable but it didn't have any errors then that
   111â†’			// suggests it was a "no-op" plan, which doesn't really do any
   112â†’			// harm to apply, so we'll just do it but leave ourselves a note
   113â†’			// in the trace log in case it ends up relevant to a bug report.
   114â†’			log.Printf("[TRACE] Applying a no-op plan")
   115â†’		} else {
   116â†’			// This situation isn't something we expect, since our own rules
   117â†’			// for what "applyable" means make this scenario impossible. We'll
   118â†’			// reject it on the assumption that something very strange is
   119â†’			// going on. and so better to halt than do something incorrect.
   120â†’			// This error message is generic and useless because we don't
   121â†’			// expect anyone to ever see it in normal use.
   122â†’			diags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(
   123â†’				tfdiags.Error,
   124â†’				"Cannot apply non-applyable plan",
   125â†’				`The given plan is not applyable. If this seems like a bug in Terraform, then please report it!`,
   126â†’			))
   127â†’			return nil, nil, diags
   128â†’		}
   129â†’	}
   130â†’
   131â†’	// The caller must provide values for all of the "apply-time variables"
   132â†’	// mentioned in the plan, and for no others because the others come from
   133â†’	// the plan itself.
   134â†’	// Apply-time variable check handled by graph walk validation.
   135â†’
   136â†’	for _, rc := range plan.Changes.Resources {
   137â†’		// Import is a no-op change during an apply (all the real action happens during the plan) but we'd
   138â†’		// like to show some helpful output that mirrors the way we show other changes.
   139â†’		if rc.Importing != nil {
   140â†’			hookResourceID := HookResourceIdentity{
   141â†’				Addr:         rc.Addr,
   142â†’				ProviderAddr: rc.ProviderAddr.Provider,
   143â†’			}
   144â†’			for _, h := range c.hooks {
   145â†’				// In future, we may need to call PostApplyImport separately elsewhere in the apply
   146â†’				// operation. For now, though, we'll call Pre and Post hooks together.
   147â†’				h.PreApplyImport(hookResourceID, *rc.Importing)
   148â†’				h.PostApplyImport(hookResourceID, *rc.Importing)
   149â†’			}
   150â†’		}
   151â†’	}

   153â†’	graph, operation, moreDiags := c.applyGraph(plan, config, opts, true)
   154â†’	diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
   155â†’	if moreDiags.HasErrors() {
   156â†’		return nil, nil, diags
   157â†’	}

   159â†’	moreDiags = checkExternalProviders(config, plan, nil, opts.ExternalProviders)
   160â†’	diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
   161â†’	if moreDiags.HasErrors() {

   163â†’	}
   164â†’
   165â†’	schemas, schemaDiags := c.Schemas(config, plan.PriorState)
   166â†’	diags = diags.Append(schemaDiags)
   167â†’	if diags.HasErrors() {
   168â†’		return nil, nil, diags
   169â†’	}

   172â†’	if err != nil {

   175â†’	}
   176â†’
   177â†’	workingState := plan.PriorState.DeepCopy()
   178â†’	walker, walkDiags := c.walk(graph, operation, &graphWalkOpts{
   179â†’		Config:                  config,
   180â†’		InputState:              workingState,
   181â†’		Changes:                 changes,
   182â†’		Overrides:               plan.Overrides,
   183â†’		ExternalProviderConfigs: opts.ExternalProviders,
   184â†’
   185â†’		DeferralAllowed: true,
   186â†’
   187â†’		// We need to propagate the check results from the plan phase,
   188â†’		// because that will tell us which checkable objects we're expecting
   189â†’		// to see updated results from during the apply step.
   190â†’		PlanTimeCheckResults: plan.Checks,
   191â†’
   192â†’		// We also want to propagate the timestamp from the plan file.
   193â†’		PlanTimeTimestamp: plan.Timestamp,
   194â†’
   195â†’		ProviderFuncResults: providers.NewFunctionResultsTable(plan.ProviderFunctionResults),

   200â†’	// After the walk is finished, we capture a simplified snapshot of the
   201â†’	// check result data as part of the new state.

   206â†’		// NOTE: This is a vestigial violation of the rule that we mustn't
   207â†’		// use plan.UIMode to affect apply-time behavior.
   208â†’		// We ideally ought to just call newState.PruneResourceHusks
   209â†’		// unconditionally here, but we historically didn't and haven't yet
   210â†’		// verified that it'd be safe to do so.
   211â†’		newState.PruneResourceHusks()

   213â†’
   214â†’	if len(plan.TargetAddrs) > 0 {
   215â†’		diags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(
   216â†’			tfdiags.Warning,
   217â†’			"Applied changes may be incomplete",
   218â†’			`The plan was created with the -target option in effect, so some changes requested in the configuration may have been ignored and the output values may not be fully updated. Run the following command to verify that no other changes are pending:
   219â†’    terraform plan
   220â†’
   221â†’Note that the -target option is not suitable for routine use, and is provided only for exceptional situations such as recovering from errors or mistakes, or when Terraform specifically suggests to use it as part of an error message.`,
   222â†’		))
   223â†’	}
   224â†’
   225â†’	// FIXME: we cannot check for an empty plan for refresh-only, because root
   226â†’	// outputs are always stored as changes. The final condition of the state
   227â†’	// also depends on some cleanup which happens during the apply walk. It
   228â†’	// would probably make more sense if applying a refresh-only plan were
   229â†’	// simply just returning the planned state and checks, but some extra
   230â†’	// cleanup is going to be needed to make the plan state match what apply
   231â†’	// would do. For now we can copy the checks over which were overwritten
   232â†’	// during the apply walk.

   234â†’	// differences in destroy plans too, so we can make use of that here as
   235â†’	// well.

   248â†’func checkApplyTimeVariables(needed collections.Set[string], gotValues InputValues, config *configs.Config) tfdiags.Diagnostics {
   249â†’	var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics
   250â†’	for name := range needed.All() {
   251â†’		if vv, exists := gotValues[name]; !exists || vv.Value == cty.NilVal || vv.Value.IsNull() {
   252â†’			// This error message assumes that the only possible reason for
   253â†’			// an apply-time variable is because the variable is ephemeral,
   254â†’			// which is true at the time of writing. This error message might
   255â†’			// need to be generalized if we introduce other reasons for
   256â†’			// apply-time variables in future.
   257â†’			diags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(
   258â†’				tfdiags.Error,
   259â†’				"No value for required variable",
   260â†’				fmt.Sprintf("The ephemeral input variable %q was set during the plan phase, and so must also be set during the apply phase.", name),
   261â†’			))
   262â†’		}
   263â†’	}
   264â†’	for name := range gotValues {
   265â†’		if !needed.Has(name) {
   266â†’			// We'll treat this a little differently depending on whether
   267â†’			// the variable is declared as ephemeral or not.
   268â†’			if vc, ok := config.Module.Variables[name]; ok && vc.Ephemeral {
   269â†’				diags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(
   270â†’					tfdiags.Error,
   271â†’					"No value for required variable",
   272â†’					fmt.Sprintf("The ephemeral input variable %q was not set during the plan phase, and so must remain unset during the apply phase.", name),
   273â†’				))
   274â†’			} else {
   275â†’				diags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(
   276â†’					tfdiags.Error,
   277â†’					"Unexpected new value for variable",
   278â†’					fmt.Sprintf("Input variable %q is non-ephemeral, so its value was fixed during planning and cannot be reset during apply.", name),
   279â†’				))
   280â†’			}
   281â†’		}
   282â†’	}
   283â†’	return diags
   284â†’}

   286â†’func (c *Context) applyGraph(plan *plans.Plan, config *configs.Config, opts *ApplyOpts, validate bool) (*Graph, walkOperation, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
   287â†’	var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics
   288â†’
   289â†’	variables := InputValues{}
   290â†’	for name, dyVal := range plan.VariableValues {
   291â†’		val, err := dyVal.Decode(cty.DynamicPseudoType)
   292â†’		if err != nil {
   293â†’			diags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(
   294â†’				tfdiags.Error,
   295â†’				"Invalid variable value in plan",
   296â†’				fmt.Sprintf("Invalid value for variable %q recorded in plan file: %s.", name, err),
   297â†’			))
   298â†’			continue
   299â†’		}
   300â†’		if pvm, ok := plan.VariableMarks[name]; ok {
   301â†’			val = val.MarkWithPaths(pvm)
   302â†’		}
   303â†’
   304â†’		variables[name] = &InputValue{
   305â†’			Value:      val,
   306â†’			SourceType: ValueFromPlan,
   307â†’		}
   308â†’	}
   309â†’	// Apply-time variables need to be merged in too.
   310â†’	// FIXME: We should check that all of these match declared variables and
   311â†’	// that all of them are declared as ephemeral, because all non-ephemeral
   312â†’	// variables are supposed to come exclusively from plan.VariableValues.
   313â†’	if opts != nil {
   314â†’		for n, vv := range opts.SetVariables {
   315â†’			variables[n] = vv
   316â†’		}
   317â†’	}
   318â†’	if diags.HasErrors() {
   319â†’		return nil, walkApply, diags
   320â†’	}
   321â†’
   322â†’	// The plan.VariableValues field only records variables that were actually
   323â†’	// set by the caller in the PlanOpts, so we may need to provide
   324â†’	// placeholders for any other variables that the user didn't set, in
   325â†’	// which case Terraform will once again use the default value from the
   326â†’	// configuration when we visit these variables during the graph walk.
   327â†’	for name := range config.Module.Variables {
   328â†’		if _, ok := variables[name]; ok {
   329â†’			continue
   330â†’		}
   331â†’		variables[name] = &InputValue{
   332â†’			Value:      cty.NilVal,
   333â†’			SourceType: ValueFromPlan,
   334â†’		}
   335â†’	}
   336â†’
   337â†’	operation := walkApply
   338â†’	if plan.UIMode == plans.DestroyMode {
   339â†’		// FIXME: Due to differences in how objects must be handled in the
   340â†’		// graph and evaluated during a complete destroy, we must continue to
   341â†’		// use plans.DestroyMode to switch on this behavior. If all objects
   342â†’		// which require special destroy handling can be tracked in the plan,
   343â†’		// then this switch will no longer be needed and we can remove the
   344â†’		// walkDestroy operation mode.
   345â†’		// TODO: Audit that and remove walkDestroy as an operation mode.
   346â†’		operation = walkDestroy
   347â†’	}
   348â†’
   349â†’	var externalProviderConfigs map[addrs.RootProviderConfig]providers.Interface
   350â†’	if opts != nil {
   351â†’		externalProviderConfigs = opts.ExternalProviders
   352â†’	}
   353â†’
   354â†’	graph, moreDiags := (&ApplyGraphBuilder{
   355â†’		Config:                  config,
   356â†’		Changes:                 plan.Changes,
   357â†’		DeferredChanges:         plan.DeferredResources,
   358â†’		State:                   plan.PriorState,
   359â†’		RootVariableValues:      variables,
   360â†’		ExternalProviderConfigs: externalProviderConfigs,
   361â†’		Plugins:                 c.plugins,
   362â†’		Targets:                 plan.TargetAddrs,
   363â†’		ForceReplace:            plan.ForceReplaceAddrs,
   364â†’		Operation:               operation,
   365â†’		ExternalReferences:      plan.ExternalReferences,
   366â†’		Overrides:               plan.Overrides,
   367â†’	}).Build(addrs.RootModuleInstance)
   368â†’	diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
   369â†’	if moreDiags.HasErrors() {
   370â†’		return nil, walkApply, diags
   371â†’	}
   372â†’
   373â†’	return graph, operation, diags
   374â†’}

   376â†’// ApplyGraphForUI is a last vestage of graphs in the public interface of
   377â†’// Context (as opposed to graphs as an implementation detail) intended only for
   378â†’// use by the "terraform graph" command when asked to render an apply-time
   379â†’// graph.
   380â†’//
   381â†’// The result of this is intended only for rendering ot the user as a dot
   382â†’// graph, and so may change in future in order to make the result more useful
   383â†’// in that context, even if drifts away from the physical graph that Terraform
   384â†’// Core currently uses as an implementation detail of applying.
   385â†’func (c *Context) ApplyGraphForUI(plan *plans.Plan, config *configs.Config) (*Graph, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
   386â†’	// For now though, this really is just the internal graph, confusing
   387â†’	// implementation details and all.
   388â†’
   389â†’	var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics
   390â†’
   391â†’	graph, _, moreDiags := c.applyGraph(plan, config, nil, false)
   392â†’	diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
   393â†’	return graph, diags
   394â†’}

See the documentation for the InputValue\n\t// type for more information on how to correctly populate this.\n\t//\n\t// During the apply phase it's only valid to specify values for input\n\t// values that were declared as ephemeral, because all other input\n\t// values must retain the values that were specified during planning.\n\tSetVariables InputValues\n}\n\n// ApplyOpts creates an [ApplyOpts] with copies of all of the elements that\n// are expected to propagate from plan to apply when planning and applying\n// in the same process.\n//\n// In practice planning and applying are often separated into two different\n// executions, in which case callers must retain enough information between\n// plan and apply to construct an equivalent [ApplyOpts] themselves without\n// using this function. This is here mainly for convenient internal use such\n// as in test cases.\nfunc (po *PlanOpts) ApplyOpts() *ApplyOpts {\n\treturn &ApplyOpts{\n\t\tExternalProviders: po.ExternalProviders,\n\t}\n}\n\n// Apply performs the actions described by the given Plan object and returns\n// the resulting updated state.\n//\n// The given configuration *must* be the same configuration that was passed\n// earlier to Context.Plan in order to create this plan.\n//\n// Even if the returned diagnostics contains errors, Apply always returns the\n// resulting state which is likely to have been partially-updated.\n//\n// The [opts] argument may be nil to indicate that no special options are\n// required. In that case, Apply will use a default set of options. Some\n// options in [PlanOpts] when creating a plan must be echoed with equivalent\n// settings during apply, so leaving opts as nil might not be valid for\n// certain combinations of plan-time options.\nfunc (c *Context) Apply(plan *plans.Plan, config *configs.Config, opts *ApplyOpts) (*states.State, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n\tstate, _, diags := c.ApplyAndEval(plan, config, opts)\n\treturn state, diags\n}\n\n// ApplyAndEval is like [Context.Apply] except that it additionally makes a\n// best effort to return a [lang.Scope] which can evaluate expressions in the\n// root module based on the content of the new state.\n//\n// The scope will be nil if the apply process doesn't complete successfully\n// enough to produce a valid evaluation scope. If the returned state is nil\n// then the scope will always be nil, but it's also possible for the scope\n// to be nil even when the state isn't, if the apply didn't complete enough for\n// the evaluation scope to produce consistent results.\nfunc (c *Context) ApplyAndEval(plan *plans.Plan, config *configs.Config, opts *ApplyOpts) (*states.State, *lang.Scope, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n\tdefer c.acquireRun(\"apply\")()\n\tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n\n\tif plan == nil {\n\t\tpanic(\"cannot apply nil plan\")\n\t}\n\tlog.Printf(\"[DEBUG] Building and walking apply graph for %s plan\", plan.UIMode)\n\n\tif opts == nil {\n\t\topts = &ApplyOpts{}\n\t}\n\n\tif plan.Errored {\n\t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n\t\t\ttfdiags.Error,\n\t\t\t\"Cannot apply failed plan\",\n\t\t\t`The given plan is incomplete due to errors during planning, and so it cannot be applied.`,\n\t\t))\n\t\treturn nil, nil, diags\n\t}\n\tif !plan.Applyable {\n\t\tif plan.Changes.Empty() {\n\t\t\t// If a plan is not applyable but it didn't have any errors then that\n\t\t\t// suggests it was a \"no-op\" plan, which doesn't really do any\n\t\t\t// harm to apply, so we'll just do it but leave ourselves a note\n\t\t\t// in the trace log in case it ends up relevant to a bug report.\n\t\t\tlog.Printf(\"[TRACE] Applying a no-op plan\")\n\t\t} else {\n\t\t\t// This situation isn't something we expect, since our own rules\n\t\t\t// for what \"applyable\" means make this scenario impossible. We'll\n\t\t\t// reject it on the assumption that something very strange is\n\t\t\t// going on. and so better to halt than do something incorrect.\n\t\t\t// This error message is generic and useless because we don't\n\t\t\t// expect anyone to ever see it in normal use.\n\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n\t\t\t\ttfdiags.Error,\n\t\t\t\t\"Cannot apply non-applyable plan\",\n\t\t\t\t`The given plan is not applyable. If this seems like a bug in Terraform, then please report it!`,\n\t\t\t))\n\t\t\treturn nil, nil, diags\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\t// The caller must provide values for all of the \"apply-time variables\"\n\t// mentioned in the plan, and for no others because the others come from\n\t// the plan itself.\n\t// Apply-time variable check handled by graph walk validation.\n\n\tfor _, rc := range plan.Changes.Resources {\n\t\t// Import is a no-op change during an apply (all the real action happens during the plan) but we'd\n\t\t// like to show some helpful output that mirrors the way we show other changes.\n\t\tif rc.Importing != nil {\n\t\t\thookResourceID := HookResourceIdentity{\n\t\t\t\tAddr:         rc.Addr,\n\t\t\t\tProviderAddr: rc.ProviderAddr.Provider,\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t\tfor _, h := range c.hooks {\n\t\t\t\t// In future, we may need to call PostApplyImport separately elsewhere in the apply\n\t\t\t\t// operation. For now, though, we'll call Pre and Post hooks together.\n\t\t\t\th.PreApplyImport(hookResourceID, *rc.Importing)\n\t\t\t\th.PostApplyImport(hookResourceID, *rc.Importing)\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\tgraph, operation, moreDiags := c.applyGraph(plan, config, opts, true)\n\tdiags = diags.Append(moreDiags)\n\tif moreDiags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn nil, nil, diags\n\t}\n\n\tmoreDiags = checkExternalProviders(config, plan, nil, opts.ExternalProviders)\n\tdiags = diags.Append(moreDiags)\n\tif moreDiags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn nil, nil, diags\n\t}\n\n\tschemas, schemaDiags := c.Schemas(config, plan.PriorState)\n\tdiags = diags.Append(schemaDiags)\n\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn nil, nil, diags\n\t}\n\n\tchanges, err := operation, &graphWalkOpts{\n\t\tConfig:                  config,\n\t\tInputState:              workingState,\n\t\tChanges:                 changes,\n\t\tOverrides:               plan.Overrides,\n\t\tExternalProviderConfigs: opts.ExternalProviders,\n\n\t\tDeferralAllowed: true,\n\n\t\t// We need to propagate the check results from the plan phase,\n\t\t// because that will tell configuration may have been ignored and the output values may not be fully updated. It\n\t// would probably make more sense if applying a refresh-only plan were\n\t// simply just returning the planned state and checks, but some extra\n\t// cleanup is going to be needed to make the plan state match what apply\n\t// would do. For now we can copy the checks over which were overwritten\n\t// during the apply walk.\n\t// Despite the intent of UIMode, it must still be used for apply-time\n\t// differences in destroy plans too, so we can make use of that here as\n\t// well.\n\tif plan.UIMode == plans.RefreshOnlyMode {\n\t\tnewState.CheckResults = plan.Checks.DeepCopy()\n\t}\n\n\t// The caller also gets access to an expression evaluation scope in the\n\t// root module, in case it needs to extract other information using\n\t// expressions, like in \"terraform console\" or the test harness.\n\tevalScope := evalScopeFromGraphWalk(walker, addrs.RootModuleInstance)\n\n\treturn newState, evalScope, diags\n}\n\nfunc checkApplyTimeVariables(needed collections.Set[string], gotValues InputValues, config *configs.Config) tfdiags.Diagnostics {\n\tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n\tfor name := range needed.All() {\n\t\tif vv, exists := gotValues[name]; !exists || vv.Value == cty.NilVal || vv.Value.IsNull() {\n\t\t\t// This error message assumes that the only possible reason for\n\t\t\t// an apply-time variable is because the variable is ephemeral,\n\t\t\t// which is true at the time of writing. This error message might\n\t\t\t// need to be generalized if we introduce other reasons for\n\t\t\t// apply-time variables in future.\n\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n\t\t\t\ttfdiags.Error,\n\t\t\t\t\"No value for required variable\",\n\t\t\t\tfmt.Sprintf(\"The ephemeral input variable %q was set during the plan phase, and so must also be set during the apply phase.\", name),\n\t\t\t))\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\tfor name := range gotValues {\n\t\tif !needed.Has(name) {\n\t\t\t// We'll treat this a little differently depending on whether\n\t\t\t// the variable is declared as ephemeral or not.\n\t\t\tif vc, ok := config.Module.Variables[name]; ok && vc.Ephemeral {\n\t\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n\t\t\t\t\ttfdiags.Error,\n\t\t\t\t\t\"No value for required variable\",\n\t\t\t\t\tfmt.Sprintf(\"The ephemeral input variable %q was not set during the plan phase, and so must remain unset during the apply phase.\", name),\n\t\t\t\t))\n\t\t\t} else {\n\t\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n\t\t\t\t\ttfdiags.Error,\n\t\t\t\t\t\"Unexpected new value for variable\",\n\t\t\t\t\tfmt.Sprintf(\"Input variable %q is non-ephemeral, so its value was fixed during planning and cannot be reset during apply.\", name),\n\t\t\t\t))\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\treturn diags\n}\n\nfunc (c *Context) applyGraph(plan *plans.Plan, config *configs.Config, opts *ApplyOpts, validate bool) (*Graph, walkOperation, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n\tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n\n\tvariables := InputValues{}\n\tfor name, dyVal := range plan.VariableValues {\n\t\tval, err := dyVal.Decode(cty.DynamicPseudoType)\n\t\tif err != nil {\n\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n\t\t\t\ttfdiags.Error,\n\t\t\t\t\"Invalid variable value in plan\",\n\t\t\t\tfmt.Sprintf(\"Invalid value for variable %q recorded in plan file: %s.\", name, err),\n\t\t\t))\n\t\t\tcontinue\n\t\t}\n\t\tif pvm, ok := plan.VariableMarks[name]; ok {\n\t\t\tval = val.MarkWithPaths(pvm)\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tvariables[name] = &InputValue{\n\t\t\tValue:      val,\n\t\t\tSourceType: ValueFromPlan,\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\t// Apply-time variables need to be merged in too.\n\t// FIXME: We should check that all of these match declared variables and\n\t// that all of them are declared as ephemeral, because all non-ephemeral\n\t// variables are supposed to come exclusively from plan.VariableValues.\n\tif opts != nil {\n\t\tfor n, vv := range opts.SetVariables {\n\t\t\tvariables[n] = vv\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn nil, walkApply, diags\n\t}\n\n\t// The plan.VariableValues field only records variables that were actually\n\t// set by the caller in the PlanOpts, so we may need to provide\n\t// placeholders for any other variables that the user didn't set, in\n\t// which case Terraform will once again use the default value from the\n\t// configuration when we visit these variables during the graph walk.\n\tfor name := range config.Module.Variables {\n\t\tif _, ok := variables[name]; ok {\n\t\t\tcontinue\n\t\t}\n\t\tvariables[name] = &InputValue{\n\t\t\tValue:      cty.NilVal,\n\t\t\tSourceType: ValueFromPlan,\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\toperation := walkApply\n\tif plan.UIMode == plans.DestroyMode {\n\t\t// FIXME: Due to differences in how objects must be handled in the\n\t\t// graph and evaluated during a complete destroy, we must continue to\n\t\t// use plans.DestroyMode to switch on this behavior. If all objects\n\t\t// which require special destroy handling can be tracked in the plan,\n\t\t// then this switch will no longer be needed and we can remove the\n\t\t// walkDestroy operation mode.\n\t\t// TODO: Audit that and remove walkDestroy as an operation mode.\n\t\toperation = walkDestroy\n\t}\n\n\tvar externalProviderConfigs map[addrs.RootProviderConfig]providers.Interface\n\tif opts != nil {\n\t\texternalProviderConfigs = opts.ExternalProviders\n\t}\n\n\tgraph, moreDiags := (&ApplyGraphBuilder{\n\t\tConfig:                  config,\n\t\tChanges:                 plan.Changes,\n\t\tDeferredChanges:         plan.DeferredResources,\n\t\tState:                   plan.PriorState,\n\t\tRootVariableValues:      variables,\n\t\tExternalProviderConfigs: externalProviderConfigs,\n\t\tPlugins:                 c.plugins,\n\t\tTargets:                 plan.TargetAddrs,\n\t\tForceReplace:            plan.ForceReplaceAddrs,\n\t\tOperation:               operation,\n\t\tExternalReferences:      plan.ExternalReferences,\n\t\tOverrides:               plan.Overrides,\n\t}).Build(addrs.RootModuleInstance)\n\tdiags = diags.Append(moreDiags)\n\tif moreDiags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn nil, walkApply, diags\n\t}\n\n\treturn graph, operation, diags\n}\n\n// ApplyGraphForUI is a last vestage of graphs in the public interface of\n// Context (as Core currently uses as an implementation detail of applying.\nfunc (c *Context) ApplyGraphForUI(plan *plans.Plan, config *configs.Config) (*Graph, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n\t// For now though, this really is just the internal graph, confusing\n\t// implementation details and all.\n\n\tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n\n\tgraph, _, moreDiags := c.applyGraph(plan, config, nil, false)\n\tdiags =      4â†’package terraform

     6â†’import (
     7â†’	"fmt"
     8â†’	"log"

    12â†’	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/addrs"

    22â†’// ApplyOpts are options that affect the details of how Terraform will apply a
    23â†’// previously-generated plan.
    24â†’type ApplyOpts struct {
    25â†’	// ExternalProviders is a set of pre-configured provider instances with
    26â†’	// the same purpose as [PlanOpts.ExternalProviders].
    27â†’	//
    28â†’	// Callers must pass providers that are configured in a similar way as
    29â†’	// the providers that were passed when creating the plan that's being
    30â†’	// applied, or the results will be erratic.
    31â†’	ExternalProviders map[addrs.RootProviderConfig]providers.Interface
    32â†’
    33â†’	// SetVariables are the raw values for root module variables as provided
    34â†’	// by the user who is requesting the run, prior to any normalization or
    35â†’	// substitution of defaults. See the documentation for the InputValue
    36â†’	// type for more information on how to correctly populate this.
    37â†’	//
    38â†’	// During the apply phase it's only valid to specify values for input
    39â†’	// values that were declared as ephemeral, because all other input
    40â†’	// values must retain the values that were specified during planning.
    41â†’	SetVariables InputValues
    42â†’}
    43â†’
    44â†’// ApplyOpts creates an [ApplyOpts] with copies of all of the elements that
    45â†’// are expected to propagate from plan to apply when planning and applying
    46â†’// in the same process.
    47â†’//
    48â†’// In practice planning and applying are often separated into two different
    49â†’// executions, in which case callers must retain enough information between
    50â†’// plan and apply to construct an equivalent [ApplyOpts] themselves without
    51â†’// using this function. This is here mainly for convenient internal use such
    52â†’// as in test cases.
    53â†’func (po *PlanOpts) ApplyOpts() *ApplyOpts {
    54â†’	return &ApplyOpts{
    55â†’		ExternalProviders: po.ExternalProviders,
    56â†’	}
    57â†’}
    58â†’
    59â†’// Apply performs the actions described by the given Plan object and returns
    60â†’// the resulting updated state.
    61â†’//
    62â†’// The given configuration *must* be the same configuration that was passed
    63â†’// earlier to Context.Plan in order to create this plan.
    64â†’//
    65â†’// Even if the returned diagnostics contains errors, Apply always returns the
    66â†’// resulting state which is likely to have been partially-updated.
    67â†’//
    68â†’// The [opts] argument may be nil to indicate that no special options are
    69â†’// required. In that case, Apply will use a default set of options. Some
    70â†’// options in [PlanOpts] when creating a plan must be echoed with equivalent
    71â†’// settings during apply, so leaving opts as nil might not be valid for
    72â†’// certain combinations of plan-time options.
    73â†’func (c *Context) Apply(plan *plans.Plan, config *configs.Config, opts *ApplyOpts) (*states.State, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
    74â†’	state, _, diags := c.ApplyAndEval(plan, config, opts)
    75â†’	return state, diags
    76â†’}
    77â†’
    78â†’// ApplyAndEval is like [Context.Apply] except that it additionally makes a
    79â†’// best effort to return a [lang.Scope] which can evaluate expressions in the
    80â†’// root module based on the content of the new state.
    81â†’//
    82â†’// The scope will be nil if the apply process doesn't complete successfully
    83â†’// enough to produce a valid evaluation scope. If the returned state is nil
    84â†’// then the scope will always be nil, but it's also possible for the scope
    85â†’// to be nil even when the state isn't, if the apply didn't complete enough for
    86â†’// the evaluation scope to produce consistent results.
    87â†’func (c *Context) ApplyAndEval(plan *plans.Plan, config *configs.Config, opts *ApplyOpts) (*states.State, *lang.Scope, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
    88â†’	defer c.acquireRun("apply")()
    89â†’	var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics
    90â†’
    91â†’	if plan == nil {
    92â†’		panic("cannot apply nil plan")
    93â†’	}
    94â†’	log.Printf("[DEBUG] Building and walking apply graph for %s plan", plan.UIMode)
    95â†’
    96â†’	if opts == nil {
    97â†’		opts = &ApplyOpts{}
    98â†’	}
    99â†’
   100â†’	if plan.Errored {
   101â†’		diags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(
   102â†’			tfdiags.Error,
   103â†’			"Cannot apply failed plan",
   104â†’			`The given plan is incomplete due to errors during planning, and so it cannot be applied.`,
   105â†’		))
   106â†’		return nil, nil, diags
   107â†’	}
   108â†’	if !plan.Applyable {
   109â†’		if plan.Changes.Empty() {
   110â†’			// If a plan is not applyable but it didn't have any errors then that
   111â†’			// suggests it was a "no-op" plan, which doesn't really do any
   112â†’			// harm to apply, so we'll just do it but leave ourselves a note
   113â†’			// in the trace log in case it ends up relevant to a bug report.
   114â†’			log.Printf("[TRACE] Applying a no-op plan")
   115â†’		} else {
   116â†’			// This situation isn't something we expect, since our own rules
   117â†’			// for what "applyable" means make this scenario impossible. We'll
   118â†’			// reject it on the assumption that something very strange is
   119â†’			// going on. and so better to halt than do something incorrect.
   120â†’			// This error message is generic and useless because we don't
   121â†’			// expect anyone to ever see it in normal use.
   122â†’			diags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(
   123â†’				tfdiags.Error,
   124â†’				"Cannot apply non-applyable plan",
   125â†’				`The given plan is not applyable. If this seems like a bug in Terraform, then please report it!`,

   138â†’		// like to show some helpful output that mirrors the way we show other changes.
   139â†’		if rc.Importing != nil {

   142â†’				ProviderAddr: rc.ProviderAddr.Provider,
   143â†’			}

   146â†’				// operation. For now, though, we'll call Pre and Post hooks together.

   150â†’		}
   151â†’	}
   152â†’
   153â†’	graph, operation, moreDiags := c.applyGraph(plan, config, opts, true)
   154â†’	diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
   155â†’	if moreDiags.HasErrors() {
   156â†’		return nil, nil, diags
   157â†’	}
   158â†’
   159â†’	moreDiags = checkExternalProviders(config, plan, nil, opts.ExternalProviders)
   160â†’	diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
   161â†’	if moreDiags.HasErrors() {

   163â†’	}
   164â†’
   165â†’	schemas, schemaDiags := c.Schemas(config, plan.PriorState)
   166â†’	diags = diags.Append(schemaDiags)
   167â†’	if diags.HasErrors() {
   168â†’		return nil, nil, diags
   169â†’	}
   170â†’
   171â†’	changes, err := plan.Changes.Decode(schemas)
   172â†’	if err != nil {
   173â†’		diags = diags.Append(err)

   175â†’	}
   176â†’
   177â†’	workingState := plan.PriorState.DeepCopy()
   178â†’	walker, walkDiags := c.walk(graph, operation, &graphWalkOpts{
   179â†’		Config:                  config,
   180â†’		InputState:              workingState,
   181â†’		Changes:                 changes,
   182â†’		Overrides:               plan.Overrides,
   183â†’		ExternalProviderConfigs: opts.ExternalProviders,
   184â†’
   185â†’		DeferralAllowed: true,
   186â†’
   187â†’		// We need to propagate the check results from the plan phase,
   188â†’		// because that will tell us which checkable objects we're expecting
   189â†’		// to see updated results from during the apply step.
   190â†’		PlanTimeCheckResults: plan.Checks,
   191â†’
   192â†’		// We also want to propagate the timestamp from the plan file.
   193â†’		PlanTimeTimestamp: plan.Timestamp,
   194â†’
   195â†’		ProviderFuncResults: providers.NewFunctionResultsTable(plan.ProviderFunctionResults),
   196â†’	})
   197â†’	diags = diags.Append(walker.NonFatalDiagnostics)
   198â†’	diags = diags.Append(walkDiags)
   199â†’
   200â†’	// After the walk is finished, we capture a simplified snapshot of the
   201â†’	// check result data as part of the new state.
   202â†’	walker.State.RecordCheckResults(walker.Checks)
   203â†’
   204â†’	newState := walker.State.Close()
   205â†’	if plan.UIMode == plans.DestroyMode && !diags.HasErrors() {
   206â†’		// NOTE: This is a vestigial violation of the rule that we mustn't
   207â†’		// use plan.UIMode to affect apply-time behavior.
   208â†’		// We ideally ought to just call newState.PruneResourceHusks
   209â†’		// unconditionally here, but we historically didn't and haven't yet
   210â†’		// verified that it'd be safe to do so.
   211â†’		newState.PruneResourceHusks()
   212â†’	}
   213â†’
   214â†’	if len(plan.TargetAddrs) > 0 {
   215â†’		diags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(
   216â†’			tfdiags.Warning,
   217â†’			"Applied changes may be incomplete",
   218â†’			`The plan was created with the -target option in effect, so some changes requested in the configuration may have been ignored and the output values may not be fully updated. Run the following command to verify that no other changes are pending:
   219â†’    terraform plan
   220â†’
   221â†’Note that the -target option is not suitable for routine use, and is provided only for exceptional situations such as recovering from errors or mistakes, or when Terraform specifically suggests to use it as part of an error message.`,
   222â†’		))
   223â†’	}
   224â†’
   225â†’	// FIXME: we cannot check for an empty plan for refresh-only, because root
   226â†’	// outputs are always stored as changes. The final condition of the state
   227â†’	// also depends on some cleanup which happens during the apply walk. It
   228â†’	// would probably make more sense if applying a refresh-only plan were
   229â†’	// simply just returning the planned state and checks, but some extra
   230â†’	// cleanup is going to be needed to make the plan state match what apply
   231â†’	// would do. For now we can copy the checks over which were overwritten
   232â†’	// during the apply walk.
   233â†’	// Despite the intent of UIMode, it must still be used for apply-time
   234â†’	// differences in destroy plans too, so we can make use of that here as
   235â†’	// well.
   236â†’	if plan.UIMode == plans.RefreshOnlyMode {
   237â†’		newState.CheckResults = plan.Checks.DeepCopy()
   238â†’	}
   239â†’
   240â†’	// The caller also gets access to an expression evaluation scope in the
   241â†’	// root module, in case it needs to extract other information using
   242â†’	// expressions, like in "terraform console" or the test harness.
   243â†’	evalScope := evalScopeFromGraphWalk(walker, addrs.RootModuleInstance)
   244â†’
   245â†’	return newState, evalScope, diags
   246â†’}

   286â†’func (c *Context) applyGraph(plan *plans.Plan, config *configs.Config, opts *ApplyOpts, validate bool) (*Graph, walkOperation, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
   287â†’	var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics

   321â†’
   322â†’	// The plan.VariableValues field only records variables that were actually
   323â†’	// set by the caller in the PlanOpts, so we may need to provide
   324â†’	// placeholders for any other variables that the user didn't set, in
   325â†’	// which case Terraform will once again use the default value from the
   326â†’	// configuration when we visit these variables during the graph walk.
   327â†’	for name := range config.Module.Variables {
   328â†’		if _, ok := variables[name]; ok {
   329â†’			continue
   330â†’		}
   331â†’		variables[name] = &InputValue{
   332â†’			Value:      cty.NilVal,
   333â†’			SourceType: ValueFromPlan,
   334â†’		}
   335â†’	}
   336â†’
   337â†’	operation := walkApply
   338â†’	if plan.UIMode == plans.DestroyMode {
   339â†’		// FIXME: Due to differences in how objects must be handled in the
   340â†’		// graph and evaluated during a complete destroy, we must continue to
   341â†’		// use plans.DestroyMode to switch on this behavior. If all objects
   342â†’		// which require special destroy handling can be tracked in the plan,
   343â†’		// then this switch will no longer be needed and we can remove the
   344â†’		// walkDestroy operation mode.
   345â†’		// TODO: Audit that and remove walkDestroy as an operation mode.
   346â†’		operation = walkDestroy
   347â†’	}
   348â†’
   349â†’	var externalProviderConfigs map[addrs.RootProviderConfig]providers.Interface
   350â†’	if opts != nil {
   351â†’		externalProviderConfigs = opts.ExternalProviders
   352â†’	}
   353â†’
   354â†’	graph, moreDiags := (&ApplyGraphBuilder{
   355â†’		Config:                  config,
   356â†’		Changes:                 plan.Changes,
   357â†’		DeferredChanges:         plan.DeferredResources,
   358â†’		State:                   plan.PriorState,
   359â†’		RootVariableValues:      variables,
   360â†’		ExternalProviderConfigs: externalProviderConfigs,
   361â†’		Plugins:                 c.plugins,
   362â†’		Targets:                 plan.TargetAddrs,
   363â†’		ForceReplace:            plan.ForceReplaceAddrs,
   364â†’		Operation:               operation,
   365â†’		ExternalReferences:      plan.ExternalReferences,
   366â†’		Overrides:               plan.Overrides,
   367â†’	}).Build(addrs.RootModuleInstance)
   368â†’	diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
   369â†’	if moreDiags.HasErrors() {
   370â†’		return nil, walkApply, diags
   371â†’	}
   372â†’
   373â†’	return graph, operation, diags
   374â†’}
   375â†’
   376â†’// ApplyGraphForUI is a last vestage of graphs in the public interface of
   377â†’// Context (as opposed to graphs as an implementation detail) intended only for
   378â†’// use by the "terraform graph" command when asked to render an apply-time
   379â†’// graph.
   380â†’//
   381â†’// The result of this is intended only for rendering ot the user as a dot
   382â†’// graph, and so may change in future in order to make the result more useful
   383â†’// in that context, even if drifts away from the physical graph that Terraform
   384â†’// Core currently uses as an implementation detail of applying.
   385â†’func (c *Context) ApplyGraphForUI(plan *plans.Plan, config *configs.Config) (*Graph, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
   386â†’	// For now though, this really is just the internal graph, confusing
   387â†’	// implementation details and all.
   388â†’
   389â†’	var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics
   390â†’
   391â†’	graph, _, moreDiags := c.applyGraph(plan, config, nil, false)
   392â†’	diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
   393â†’	return graph, diags
   394â†’}

     6â†’import (
     7â†’	"fmt"
     8â†’	"log"
     9â†’
    10â†’	"github.com/zclconf/go-cty/cty"
    11â†’
    12â†’	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/addrs"
    13â†’	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/collections"
    14â†’	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/configs"
